description: Explains how pytest's top-level public API is assembled, where the reference documentation points users, and how key exported helpers for comparisons, exceptions, outcomes, and warnings behave.
document:
Pytest presents most user-facing functionality through the root package, so test code normally imports one namespace and reaches supported helpers from there. The file `src/pytest/__init__.py` is the source declaration of that public surface: it imports implementations from internal modules, assigns the debugger entry point, and publishes the supported names through its export list. The interface is broad enough to include marks, fixtures, collection and report objects, plugin hooks, warning classes, outcome helpers, approximate comparison support, exception assertions, and warning assertions, but the implementation details remain in `_pytest` modules. The reference page mirrors this by documenting the root-level constants, functions, marks, fixtures, hooks, and exported objects under the pytest namespace rather than asking users to import internals.

The API reference emphasizes a small set of everyday functions. It documents version information, parametrization support, assertion rewriting registration, command-line entry points, and the helpers used inside tests. The older built-in page mostly redirects users to the main API reference, while still showing that fixture discovery is available from the command line and describing public built-in fixtures such as the warning recorder. This split makes the public interface a stable facade over many internal subsystems: source code gathers the names, and documentation explains the intended way to reach them.

Approximate comparison is implemented in `src/_pytest/python_api.py` and exposed as `pytest.approx` for assertions where exact equality is too strict. The public helper dispatches on the expected value, choosing specialized comparison objects for scalars, decimal values, mappings, ordered sequences, and NumPy arrays when NumPy is already imported. All of those forms delegate scalar tolerance decisions to the same core comparison behavior. By default it accepts values close to the expected value using a relative tolerance plus a very small absolute tolerance, treats infinity only as equal to itself, and treats NaN as unequal unless the caller opts in. Ordered containers are compared element by element or value by value, mappings must have matching keys, unordered collections are rejected, and non-numeric values fall back to strict equality.

Exception assertion support lives in `src/_pytest/raises.py`. The public `pytest.raises` helper supports both context-manager and function-call styles. In context-manager form it returns an object that later fills an exception-info container if the raised exception matches the expected type, optional regex, and optional predicate check. Matching uses subclass checks rather than exact-type checks, searches the formatted exception text, and runs the predicate only after type and text matching have succeeded. For exception groups, the exported helpers model stricter structure checking than a simple containment test: the group helper expects all specified nested exceptions and no unexpected ones, with options for flattening nested groups or accepting a single unwrapped exception in limited cases.

Outcome helpers are defined in `src/_pytest/outcomes.py` and exported as callable objects rather than plain functions so each one can carry its public exception class. The fail, skip, xfail, and exit helpers all work by raising dedicated exceptions that pytest interprets as test outcomes or immediate process exit. The import-or-skip helper first imports a named module, turns selected import failures into a skip, and can also enforce a minimum version before returning the imported module.

Warning assertion support is implemented in `src/_pytest/recwarn.py`. The public `pytest.warns` helper can be used as a context manager or around a callable; it records all emitted warnings, requires at least one warning of the expected category, and optionally checks warning text with a regex. Unmatched warnings are re-emitted when the context closes. The related deprecation helper narrows the expected categories to the deprecation-style warnings, and the built-in `recwarn` fixture yields a reusable recorder for tests that need to inspect, pop, clear, or iterate over captured warning messages directly.
